title: Understand Area as Covering with Unit Squares
essential_question: What is area, and how can you measure it with unit squares?

pretest.question: What is the AREA of a flat shape?
choices: A) The distance around the shape
B) The number of unit squares that cover it

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.1 'Understand Area'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Concept of Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015
verification.original_ref: Area = unit squares that cover the shape.

hints: Area is about COVERING the inside of a shape.

feedback.correct: Yes! Area is the number of unit squares that cover a shape.
feedback.incorrect: Area = the number of unit squares that cover a shape.

pretest.feedback_correct: Yes! Area is the number of unit squares that cover a shape.

pretest.question: What is a UNIT SQUARE?
choices: A) A square with sides 1 unit long

C.note: Unit squares are squares, not circles

D.note: Unit squares are 2D — squares, not lines

hints: The 'unit' tells you the side length.

feedback.correct: Yes! A unit square has sides 1 unit long.
feedback.incorrect: A unit square has all sides 1 unit long.

pretest.feedback_correct: Yes! A unit square has sides 1 unit long.

pretest.question: When measuring area with unit squares, the squares must:
choices: C) Cover with no gaps and no overlaps

D.note: Unit squares must be the same size

hints: Gaps would skip area; overlaps would double-count.

feedback.correct: Yes! No gaps and no overlaps.
feedback.incorrect: Unit squares must cover with no gaps and no overlaps.

pretest.feedback_correct: Yes! No gaps and no overlaps.

verification.original_ref: Area unit: square cm (or sq cm).

pretest.feedback_correct: Yes! Square cm is a unit for area.

learn.title: What Is Area?
learn.content: AREA is the amount of space INSIDE a flat shape. We can think of area as how much paper, paint, or tile is needed to cover a shape with no gaps. Area answers the question 'how much space does this shape cover?'

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.1 'Understand Area'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Concept of Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Unit Squares
learn.content: A UNIT SQUARE is a square with all sides 1 unit long. We use unit squares to MEASURE area. Common unit squares: 1 cm × 1 cm = 1 square cm. 1 inch × 1 inch = 1 square inch. 1 m × 1 m = 1 square meter.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.1 'Understand Area'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Concept of Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Covering Without Gaps or Overlaps
learn.content: To find area, we cover a shape with unit squares. RULES: (1) NO GAPS — every part of the shape must be covered. (2) NO OVERLAPS — squares cannot stack on each other. Every unit square counts as 1 square unit of area.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.1 'Understand Area'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Concept of Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Counting Unit Squares
learn.content: After covering a shape with unit squares (no gaps, no overlaps), count them. The total count is the AREA in SQUARE UNITS. Example: a rectangle covered by 12 unit squares has area = 12 square units.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.1 'Understand Area'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Concept of Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Area Units
learn.content: Area is measured in SQUARE UNITS — square centimeters (sq cm), square inches (sq in), square meters (sq m), square feet (sq ft). The word 'square' is important: it means we are measuring area, not length.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.1 'Understand Area'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Concept of Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Area vs Length
learn.content: LENGTH is how far across (one direction): cm, m, inches. AREA is how much surface (two directions): sq cm, sq m, sq inches. A line has length but no area. A rectangle has both length AND width — together they tell you about area.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.1 'Understand Area'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Concept of Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015
verification.original_ref: Go Math 11.4: distinguish area from length.

learn.title: Bigger Shapes Need More Squares
learn.content: A bigger shape needs MORE unit squares to cover it. A small sticky note might be 9 sq cm. A book cover might be 400 sq cm. A school playground might be 1,000 square meters. Bigger shape = more area = more unit squares.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.1 'Understand Area'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Concept of Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Lesson Summary
learn.content: Today you learned: (1) AREA = space inside a flat shape. (2) UNIT SQUARE = 1×1 square. (3) Cover with NO GAPS, NO OVERLAPS. (4) Count unit squares to find area. (5) Area uses SQUARE UNITS (sq cm, sq m, etc.). (6) Bigger shape = more area.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.1 'Understand Area'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Concept of Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

try.question: Tom places unit squares on a shape, but some squares overlap each other. Will he get the correct area?
choices: A) Yes — overlaps count as 1 square
B) No — overlaps make the count too high
C) No — overlaps make the count too low
D) Yes, only if all squares overlap
solution_steps: Step 1: Overlapping squares are counted twice.
Step 2: Count is too high → wrong area.

feedback.correct: Right! Overlaps make the count too high.
feedback.incorrect: Overlaps mean double-counting, so the count is too high.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.11 Lesson 11.1 'Understand Area'
verification.procedure_source: EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Concept of Area
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: What happens if you count the same square twice?
try.feedback_correct: Right! Overlaps make the count too high.

hints: Multiply rows by squares per row.

try.question: A rectangle has area 24. What unit should you use?
solution_steps: Step 2: 24 sq cm is a valid area.

feedback.incorrect: Area is measured in square units like square cm.

feedback.correct: False! Distance around is perimeter. Area is the inside.
feedback.incorrect: False. The distance around is perimeter. Area = inside space.

practice_r3.question: Lily covers a shape with 12 unit squares but ONE overlaps another. Her count says area = 12 sq units. What is the actual area?
solution_steps: Step 2: 1 overlaps another → 1 square is counted twice.

A.note: Overlap means count is too high

feedback.incorrect: 1 overlap means 1 fewer real square — 12 - 1 = 11 sq units.

practice_r3.question: Mia's poster is 4 unit squares wide and 6 unit squares tall. She buys a frame that covers an extra 1 unit square all around. What is the area of the framed poster (poster + frame)?
solution_steps: Step 2: With 1 unit on every side: width becomes 4 + 2 = 6.

A.note: That is the poster only — add frame

C.note: 5×7=35 but width and height each grow by 2 not 1

feedback.incorrect: Frame adds 1 unit on EACH side — both dimensions grow by 2. (4+2)×(6+2)=48.
